findings.claim: R-RP definition requires output invariance within L_p ball, but invariance does not prevent inference that x₁ ∈ [z-R, z+R]. No formal bound on I(x₁; f(x)) or adversarial posterior advantage.
findings.evidence: Counterexample: if f invariant on [z-R, z+R], adversary observing output learns x₁ is in [z-R, z+R] — entropy reduction proportional to 2R / prior support. APE definitions compound this by defining privacy via expanded inference interval.
findings.decision_impact: Paper presents RP as a privacy framework, but the formal machinery shows only that output invariance exists. The privacy claim is definitional, not theorematic.

findings.claim: Single MIA method (Kahla et al. 2022, label-only) evaluated. No adaptive adversary. No gradient-based/GAN-based MIA. Baseline (random abstention at 40% rate) not tested.
findings.evidence: ASR 73%→4% at σ=0.1 requires accuracy 100%→59.4%. Random 40% abstention achieves similar ASR reduction without RS infrastructure. Strongest mitigation result (σ=0.03, ASR=44%) still constitutes functional success for inference adversary.
findings.decision_impact: Cannot isolate certified robustness contribution from simple output suppression. Headline 4% ASR requires impractical accuracy degradation.

verdict_state.rationale: Interesting analogy connecting certified robustness to inference-time privacy. But formal gap: output invariance ≠ inference prevention. Narrow MIA evaluation (one method, non-adaptive). Severe utility-privacy trade-off at headline claim. No comparison to simpler baselines or DP-trained models. Evaluation domain (face recognition, medical insurance) is narrow.
